entries.definition: worthy of respect because of advanced age, dignity, etc.
entries.sentence: At family reunions our venerable grandmother, now past eighty, is accorded great respect.

entries.definition: foolish for a grown person to say or do; childish
entries.sentence: Some seniors think it's fun to throw objects at passing cars, but I consider it puerile.

entries.definition: lack of proper seriousness; improper gaiety
entries.sentence: During the assembly George kept giggling; levity for which his teacherlater scolded him.

entries.definition: thrifty; not wasteful
entries.sentence: Her frugal attitude allowed her to save a good deal of money at the supermarket.

entries.definition: feeling of intense dislike
entries.sentence: Because of his bigoted remarks, the speaker provoked the antipathy of theaudience.

entries.definition: a hobby; supplementary occupation
entries.sentence: Bird watching was an avocation thatgave him many hours of pleasure.

entries.definition: one who poses as an expert in a skill or profession for which he isnot qualified
entries.sentence: The Great Imposter is a movie about a charlatan who posed successfully as a surgeon.

entries.definition: brave; strong and able
entries.sentence: The doughty naval commander foughtagainst very heavy odds.

entries.definition: to make clear; explain
entries.sentence: You may elucidate what happened afteryou relax for a while.

entries.definition: lazy
entries.sentence: He was indolent by nature but still blamed the heat wave for his inabilityto do work.

entries.definition: causing laughter because absurd or ridiculous; laughably absurd
entries.sentence: His answer to the question was so ludicrous that everyone burst outlaughing.

entries.definition: young and inexperienced; immature
entries.sentence: He was surprised when his companyhired a callow youth just out of college for the important new position.

entries.definition: merry; cheerful; happy
entries.sentence: Our neighbor is a blithe fellow whotells amusing stories.

entries.definition: favoritism shown to relatives, especially in securing jobs
entries.sentence: Whenever a President appoints a relative to a government position, the cry of nepotism is raised by theopposing party.

entries.definition: to speak evil of; toslander
entries.sentence: I cannot bear to hear you malign sucha good man.

entries.definition: occurring after death; published after the author's death
entries.sentence: Only two of Emily Dickinson’s poemswere published before she died; the rest were posthumous.

entries.definition: outrageously evil orwicked
entries.sentence: The convict was severely punished forhis heinous crime.

entries.definition: kept secret or hidden especially for someillegal purpose; secretive
entries.sentence: The clandestine activities of the terrorist group were never discoveredby the police.

entries.definition: to disprove or demonstrate the falsity ofsomething
entries.sentence: The district attorney summoned many witnesses to refute the testimony of thedefendant.

entries.definition: hasty, hurried or not thorough
entries.sentence: His cursory studying of the materialcould not give him a full understanding of the subject.

entries.definition: quiet, reserved, or uncommunicative
entries.sentence: Because she was so reticent, very fewpeople got to know her well.

entries.definition: hard work; exhausting labor; agony
entries.sentence: His family found that the travail ofserving ten years in a prison camp had changed him greatly.

entries.definition: wealth, luxury, orabundance.
entries.sentence: She was amazed at the opulence of herneighbors.

entries.definition: difficult, strenuous, or hard to achieve
entries.sentence: Climbing the tree proved to be anarduous task but not a particularly dangerous one.

entries.definition: practically worthless; petty; trifling
entries.sentence: I complained not because of the paltrythree cents that I was overcharged but because of the principle of the thing.

entries.definition: to carry off by force
entries.sentence: The kidnappers abducted the childfrom her home.

entries.definition: easily managed, taught, or controlled
entries.sentence: The horse was tractable as long as he was going in the direction of the barn; otherwise he was unmanageable.

entries.definition: to do away with or destroy; to blot outcompletely
entries.sentence: He tried to obliterate from his memory any trace of that horrible day.

entries.definition: extraordinary in size oramount; enormous
entries.sentence: Anyone who has orbited the Earth hasperformed a prodigious task.

entries.definition: having or showing great pride in oneself and disdain, contempt, orscorn for others
entries.sentence: He seemed at first to be rather cold and haughty, but we came to realize that this was due mainly to his shyness.

entries.definition: Evenness of mind or temper; calmness; composure
entries.sentence: When one becomes engaged in an argument it is best to retain one’s equanimity in order to keep the disputefrom exploding into something bigger.

entries.definition: To irritate or make bitter; to make more violent
entries.sentence: The line of questions so exacerbated the witness that he refused to answer.

entries.definition: A tip; payment for service for which no billis offered
entries.sentence: A gratuity of 10 to 15 percent of the check is customary in many restaurants.

entries.definition: Skeptical; doubtful; too extraordinary to be believed
entries.sentence: The teacher was incredulous when he heard the amazing excuse.

entries.definition: Talkative; chatty
entries.sentence: The lawyer’s loquacious summation annoyed his client, who could see thatthe jury was losing attention.

entries.definition: Deserving blame; wrong
entries.sentence: The man did not find the action of hisenemy as reprehensible as he thought he would.

entries.definition: Strict; rigid; exacting
entries.sentence: The Dean imposed a curfew of 7:00, the most stringent one in the history of the school.

entries.definition: Habitually silent; notgiven to conversation
entries.sentence: John Wayne often portrays taciturn characters in his movies.

entries.definition: Present everywhere
entries.sentence: The ubiquitous pizza parlors seem tobe feeding America.

entries.definition: Having lost its spirit or zest; spiritless
entries.sentence: The guest speaker made one of the most vapid speeches the audience hadever heard.

entries.definition: Overly or unduly careful about small details; extremely careful
entries.sentence: She was extremely meticulous about her clothes and spent hours dressingfor a party.

entries.definition: Unselfish concern for the welfare of others; selflessness
entries.sentence: Altruism is a virtue that very few people possess.

entries.definition: To improve or make better
entries.sentence: Despite the Mayor's best efforts, hecould not ameliorate the situation in the ghetto.

entries.definition: Characteristic of the country and country people; simple and unsophisticated
entries.sentence: His rustic manners seemed out of place in the elegant setting of the hotel dining room.

entries.definition: To make fun of; to scorn
entries.sentence: The teacher told the obnoxious studentnot to deride his fellow classmates.

entries.definition: Lacking self confidence; timid; shy
entries.sentence: We discovered that beneath hisdiffident Personality there was an active and inquiring mind.

entries.definition: To exclude someone from a social group by general consent
entries.sentence: Although he was found innocent of treason, Aaron Burr was ostracized by society and left this country to live in England.

entries.definition: Savage; cruel; fierce; warlike
entries.sentence: The debate, which might have beenvery interesting, was marred by a truculent exchange of personal abuse.

entries.definition: To clear of suspicion, asof a charge of wrongdoing
entries.sentence: Since he has been fully vindicated bythe investigation, he will resume his old position in the government.

entries.definition: Eager and ready to fight; quarrelsome
entries.sentence: He became much less pugnacious afterhe picked fights with boys who gave him sound beatings.

entries.definition: looking dangerous, threatening, disagreeable, repellent
entries.sentence: The glow from the distant lighthouse cast a forbidding hue over the ocean.

entries.definition: foolish, silly, lacking sense or meaning
entries.sentence: We were trying not to sound inane aswe spoke to the art historian, but we really knew nothing about the subject.

entries.definition: to become accustomed to something, difficult, painful, etc.
entries.sentence: I never became inured to running in the early morning.

entries.definition: the complete absence of government, political disorder or violence, disorder in any activity
entries.sentence: “If he gets up, we’ll all get up. It’ll be anarchy,” is a famous line from The Breakfast Club.

entries.definition: hostility, antagonism, hatred; the bitter feelingsof an enemy
entries.sentence: Many Americans cannot help but feel enmity toward Osama Bin Laden and his terrorist network.

entries.definition: any office or position that brings profit or advantage without involving muchwork or responsibility
entries.sentence: No one would ever need to retire from a job that is a sinecure.

entries.definition: not expressed or declared openly, but implied or understood
entries.sentence: Her nod was enough tacit approval for me to understand her message.

entries.definition: to trick into doing something, to lead onwith deception
entries.sentence: The salesman had a reputation for inveigling his customers into buying things they did not intend to buy.

entries.definition: anything that is or seemsto be out of its proper time in history
entries.sentence: For a girl to call her boyfriend her “beau” is anachronistic in 2011.

entries.definition: too great a desire to have wealth; greed for riches
entries.sentence: His avarice increased with age, until it became an uncontrollable obsessionfor wealth.

entries.definition: to excuse, forgive, or pardon; to overlook anoffense
entries.sentence: His behavior was so outrageous that I cannot see how anyone could even tryto condone it.

entries.definition: made trite and commonplace by overuse; stale
entries.sentence: His writing style is weakened by repeated use of hackneyed phrases.

entries.definition: required; absolutely necessary; compulsory
entries.sentence: It is imperative that you submit your application before May 1.

entries.definition: worldly, (as distinguished from heavenly); commonplace
entries.sentence: Now that we have discussed all those profound philosophical problems, let's get down to the mundane job ofpreparing dinner.

entries.definition: calm; peaceful; undisturbed
entries.sentence: Although the scene was still placid, I knew that before long we were in forsome wild excitement.

entries.definition: not genuine or true; lacking in authenticity; fake
entries.sentence: The document on which we hadintended to base our case turned out to be spurious--a crude counterfeit.

entries.definition: marked by secrecy or concealment; furtive; sly
entries.sentence: Creeping stealthily, through theunderbrush, the soldiers approached their enemies.

entries.definition: refined; polite andcourteous in a smooth, polished way; suave
entries.sentence: I admired the urbane ease with which he handled the difficult situation.

entries.definition: a social outcast; one despised by society
entries.sentence: In Lord of the Flies, Piggy quickly became the pariah of the small societythat formed on the island.

entries.definition: to violate the sacredness of something; to profane; to treat as if not sacred
entries.sentence: Vandals had desecrated the church by carving their initials on the largewooden doors.

entries.definition: loud, noisy, or vehement in making one's feelingsknown
entries.sentence: The judge ordered the vociferous spectator to be thrown out of the courtroom

entries.definition: a mild or pleasant expression substituted forone that is harsh or blunt
entries.sentence: Instead of calling Meredith "fat," hetactfully employed the euphemism "pleasingly plump."

entries.definition: a scarcity or lack; too small a supply or shortage
entries.sentence: When the crops failed, there was adearth of grain for flour, and bread prices skyrocketed.

entries.definition: a person or thing that comes before to announce or give an indication ofwhat is to follow
entries.sentence: The first robin is a well-known harbinger of spring. The first vulture circling is a harbinger of something else.

entries.definition: angry, wrathful, incensed
entries.sentence: He is usually even-tempered, but he becomes irate when he encounters rudeness.

entries.definition: the highest point, peak; culmination
entries.sentence: At the very pinnacle of success, he enjoyed wealth, admiration from others, and widespread affection.

entries.definition: stubbornly disobedient or rebellious; hard to handle; unruly
entries.sentence: We cannot allow one recalcitrant student to disrupt the work of the entire class.

entries.definition: dirty from neglect; wretched
entries.sentence: Jakob Riis photographed the awful living conditions in the squalid tenements of New York.

entries.definition: unclear; vague; cloudy, misty
entries.sentence: His ideas were so nebulous that no one could agree or disagree with him.

entries.definition: to speak out against strongly and openly; todenounce
entries.sentence: The graduation speaker decried the tendency of too many Americans to seek wealth before all else.

entries.definition: to praise highly; to laud
entries.sentence: The speaker extolled the doctor for hisgreat contributions to mankind.

entries.definition: that which seems to be with someone from birth; existing naturally ratherthan acquired
entries.sentence: His good taste and courtesy are not the result of training but rather are innate.

entries.definition: a perplexing or seemingly unexplainable matter orperson
entries.sentence: The many aspects of his changing personality make Tom an enigma to me.

entries.definition: a continuing and bitterhate or ill will; deep spite and malice.
entries.sentence: Unfortunately, the debate between them was marked by personal rancor ratherthan honest discussion of the issues.

entries.definition: a violent verbal attack; strong criticism, insultcurses, etc.
entries.sentence: It is possible to wage a hard-hitting political campaign without resorting tothe use of invectives.

entries.definition: keenness and quickness in understanding and dealing with a situation; shrewdness
entries.sentence: In the 19th century, many fortunes were made by men with great business acumen.

entries.definition: not easily moved to pity or sympathy; hard- hearted, stubborn orobstinate
entries.sentence: In spite of all our efforts to persuade him, he remained obdurate.

entries.definition: practical; concerned with actual, everyday things rather than with theory orspeculation
entries.sentence: Rather than listening to the politicians explain the theories of poverty, he had the pragmatic problem of getting foodon the table.

entries.definition: fearlessly daring; bold; arrogantly insolent
entries.sentence: In this time of crisis, we need imaginative, bold and even audacious leadership.

entries.definition: common, unoriginal, drearily predictable
entries.sentence: The lecturer’s banal comments boredthe audience to tears.

entries.definition: Responsible for doing something wrong; deserving blame; guilty
entries.sentence: Anyone who took any part in the conspiracy was considered equally culpable.

entries.definition: Deserving disgrace or shame; dishonorable; despicable
entries.sentence: The traitor spent the last years of his life in ignominious exile.

entries.definition: showing off boastfully; boastful
entries.sentence: The man displayed his expensive newsuit in a very ostentatious manner.

entries.definition: changes in fortune; ups and downs in circumstances
entries.sentence: You must learn to face the many vicissitudes of life.

entries.definition: cheerful willingness or eagerness
entries.sentence: Since Bob needed a job to pay for his college expenses, he accepted withalacrity the offer.

entries.definition: in a state of decline or decay; deteriorating
entries.sentence: When people lose interest in literature, it's a sign that the society is becoming decadent.

entries.definition: Talkative
entries.sentence: The garrulous cab driver kept up a steady stream of chatter.

entries.definition: Reckless, boldness; foolish disregard for danger
entries.sentence: In 1941, Japanese militarists had the temerity to risk the future of their country on the wild gamble of aknockout blow on Pearl Harbor.

entries.definition: composed of materials gained from many sources, systems, etc.
entries.sentence: The statesman did no believe in only one political philosophy, but rather took a more eclectic view of worldaffairs.

entries.definition: a thing or person accursed or damned; a thing or person greatly detested; a formal curse
entries.sentence: After almost 200 years, the name of Benedict Arnold is still anathema to Americans.

entries.definition: noble in mind; high-souled; generous in overlooking injury or insult; rising above pettiness ormeanness
entries.sentence: Churchill emphasized that we should be firm in war, defiant in defeat, and magnanimous in victory.

entries.definition: a person who hates or mistrusts all people
entries.sentence: Because of his hateful stares and habitual silence, the man had the reputation of being a misanthrope.

entries.definition: unreasonably cheap or stingy
entries.sentence: He had become so accustomed to a parsimonious way of living that he deniedhimself even the simplest comforts.

entries.definition: a person who has or professes to have great learning; actual or self-professed authority
entries.sentence: During the week of the Super Bowl, many so-called pundits are always telling you in advance which team will win the game.

entries.definition: offering unnecessary or unwanted advice or services; meddlesome, especially in an overbearing way
entries.sentence: To disguise the fact that he was performing no useful function, he developed an officious manner ofintruding into everyone else’s work

entries.definition: bold; fearless; dauntless; very brave
entries.sentence: All honors should go to the intrepid andhighly trained men who are exploring outer space.

entries.definition: very sad or mournful, especially in a way that seems exaggerated orridiculous
entries.sentence: His lugubrious expression was intended to suggest that he, more than anyone else, understood the sadness of the occasion.

entries.definition: intended for or understood by only a chosen few beyond the knowledge and understanding of mostpeople
entries.sentence: The lecture was too esoteric to be appreciated by most people in the audience.

entries.definition: arrogant; haughty; seeking to dominate; overbearing
entries.sentence: He is so inflated with the sense of his ownimportance that he adopts an imperious manner toward his subordinates.

entries.definition: distressing to the feelings; touching; appealing to the emotions; affecting
entries.sentence: Who can remain unmoved by thepoignant scene in which Sidney Carton sacrifices his life for the woman he loves.

entries.definition: distasteful; offensive; repulsive
entries.sentence: I find his horrible manners utterly repugnant.

entries.definition: implied but not expressed
entries.sentence: Since mother raised no objection to myidea, I assumed that I had her tacit consent to go ahead with the plan.

entries.definition: involving sympathetic participation by aperson in the experiences of another; experienced or endured or enjoyed through imaginative participation in the experiences of another
entries.sentence: Having been too frail to participate in competitive sports, he took vicarious pleasure in his younger brother's football heroics.

entries.definition: to evade or conceal the truth; to deviate from the truth; to lie
entries.sentence: Although you may be tempted to prevaricate, in the long run it will be better off if you own up to what youdid.

entries.definition: contemptuously proud; haughty; disdainful
entries.sentence: In spite of his supercilious attitude, Irefuse to concede that he is our social superior.

entries.definition: to weaken; to deprive offorce
entries.sentence: The long stretch of hot, humid weather enervated all of us.

entries.definition: an excess; an overabundance
entries.sentence: The boy has a plethora of energy and inventiveness, which he sometimes directs into mischief.

entries.definition: an original that serves as a model on which later stages are based or judged; an early andtypical example
entries.sentence: Salinger's brilliant Catcher in the Rye has served as the prototype for many novels about confused teenagers.

entries.definition: to one side; crookedly
entries.sentence: In my efforts to straighten the picture, I knocked the lampshade askew.

entries.definition: to cancel or repeal by authority; to annul
entries.sentence: Since the treaty had proved unworkable, the two nations decided to abrogate it.

entries.definition: to leave to another in a will; to hand down
entries.sentence: My father bequeathed to his children a little money and the glorious example of a life well spent in the service of hiscountry.

entries.definition: to praise highly
entries.sentence: At the testimonial dinner, he was lauded by community leaders for hisservice to the school.

entries.definition: grossly stupid or dull; coarse
entries.sentence: His attempts to argue about a subjectfar beyond his depth seemed only to reveal his crass ignorance and conceit.

entries.definition: smug; self satisfied; pleased with oneself
entries.sentence: He had the complacent attitude of a man who considers himself superior tothe "common herd."

entries.definition: great misery or distress; great trouble
entries.sentence: The tribulations which he suffered and overcame have made him a wiser, more compassionate person.

entries.definition: to coax or persuade with insincere or falsewords; to wheedle
entries.sentence: Using rather far-fetched promises, I finally cajoled her into accompanying us tothe fair

entries.definition: mental distress due to disappointment orhumiliation
entries.sentence: When he did not receive an invitation, he tried unsuccessfully to conceal hisdeep-seated chagrin.

entries.definition: easily taught; responsive; tractable
entries.sentence: Since I expected him to be unruly, Iwas pleasantly surprised by his gentle, docile behavior.

entries.definition: to waver in mind or opinion
entries.sentence: When you have considered all the factors carefully and have decidedwhat is right, act without vacillating.

entries.definition: marked by wisdom andgood judgment
entries.sentence: His sage advice has helped thousandsof people deal with their problems.

entries.definition: to deceive
entries.sentence: Only an inexperienced and naive person could be duped into investing money in such a hairbrained scheme.

entries.definition: highest point; pinnacle
entries.sentence: Election to the Presidency was theacme of his long and brilliant career.

entries.definition: to restrain or discourage from action through fear
entries.sentence: Once he had decided where his dutylay, nothing could deter him.

entries.definition: a sermon; a discourse emphasizing moral principles
entries.sentence: Instead of those high-flown homilies, we need some good practical plans.

entries.definition: one who cultivates refined tastes, especially in eatingand drinking
entries.sentence: The store specializes in delicacies thatwill please even the most demanding epicures.

entries.definition: excessive amount
entries.sentence: I have had my surfeit of excuses andevasions; I want action!

entries.definition: traveling from place to place
entries.sentence: Peter became an itinerant folk singer, traveling to college campuses all overthe land.

entries.definition: a hiding place
entries.sentence: The conspirators had set up a cache offirearms in the old farmhouse.

entries.definition: to instigate; to spur on
entries.sentence: Only intolerable living conditions couldhave incited the peasants to rebellion.

entries.definition: to bring to a state of peace or quiet; to satisfy by making concessions orgiving in to
entries.sentence: The tyrant can be appeased only by absolute submission to his will.

entries.definition: impulsive; done hastily or rashly
entries.sentence: Because he is impetuous by nature, John often has cause to regret his rashactions.

entries.definition: treachery; breach of trust or faith
entries.sentence: Because he betrayed our country, Benedict Arnold's name has become asymbol of perfidy in American history.

entries.definition: a dirty, wretched dwelling place
entries.sentence: It is shocking that the families of the migratory workers had to live in suchhovels.

entries.definition: coming into view orbeing; arrival
entries.sentence: The advent of winter brings thoughts ofsleds, ice-skates, and skis.

entries.definition: needy; poor
entries.sentence: The purpose of this new program is tohelp those indigent people who can't help themselves.

entries.definition: impudence; shameless boldness
entries.sentence: How does he have the effrontery to askme for help when he ignored my requests during my time of need.

entries.definition: to correct; to set right
entries.sentence: It is good to rectify a mistake; it isbetter not to make it in the first place.

entries.definition: to accumulate; to gather together
entries.sentence: By saving systematically over the years, he amassed a large fortune.
